claim.text: Taxila (Takṣaśilā) was an ancient university — often 'the world's first university'.
claim.asCirculated: "Taxila university, one of the world's greatest centers of learning" / "the world's first university, established 700 BCE"

whatsTrue: Taxila was a genuinely famous center of learning across Buddhist and Brahmanical sources — Jātakas send princes and physicians (Jīvaka) there to study; Pāṇini is associated with the region; the excavated city complexes are a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
whatsDistorted: 'University' implies a corporate institution — shared campus, admission, degrees, faculties. The sources describe teacher-centered gurukula-style schools: individual masters hosting students in their homes. Nālandā (5th c. CE) IS institutional — endowed, residential, corporate — which is why precision matters: inflating Taxila cheapens the real institutional claim Nālandā has.

supporting.point: Textual fame as a learning destination is real and multi-source (Pāli Jātakas, Sanskrit sources, Greek accounts of the region).

sources.label: Marshall, J., Taxila: An Illustrated Account of Archaeological Excavations, 3 vols. (Cambridge, 1951)

countering.point: Scharfe's standard study concludes Taxila had no institutional organization — 'university' is a modern projection; education there was private and teacher-run.

sources.label: Scharfe, H., Education in Ancient India (Brill, 2002), ch. on Taxila
Altekar, A.S., Education in Ancient India (1944) — earlier, similar assessment

verdict.rationale: Used by boosters on multiple sides (it appears in both Indian 'first university' listicles and the 'ancient Pakistan' posts). The accurate sentence — 'Taxila was among the ancient world's most famous hubs of higher learning' — needs no inflation.

sources.label: Scharfe, H., Education in Ancient India (2002)
Altekar, A.S., Education in Ancient India (1944)

quickRebuttal: Taxila was a famed hub of individual teacher-run schools — students lived with masters, no campus, no degrees, no corporation (Scharfe, Education in Ancient India, 2002). The institutional-university title belongs to Nālandā (5th c. CE: endowed, residential, admission exams). The accurate sentence — 'among the ancient world's most famous centers of learning' — needs no inflation.

spread.note: Used by both Indian 'first university' listicles and 'ancient Pakistan' posts — same inflation, opposite flags.
knownVariants: Takshashila, the world's first university, established 700 BCE
